2025 · arXiv · Benchmarks & Evals

GDPval: Evaluating AI Model Performance on Real-World Economically Valuable Tasks

A 1,320-task benchmark of real, paid knowledge work spanning 44 occupations in the nine largest U.S. GDP sectors — graded not against an answer key but by blinded expert pairwise comparison against an industry professional's own deliverable. The first time frontier models are measured head-to-head against the people whose work they might do.

Patwardhan, Dias, Proehl, Kim, Wang, Watkins, Fishman, … Barr, Glaese, Tworek · OpenAI
venue arXiv citations 73 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-06-19
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

The debate over how AI will reshape the labor market is being fought with lagging indicators: adoption rates, usage logs, GDP-growth attribution. GDPval's authors argue these arrive too late — history says the leap from invention to economy-wide use takes years or decades (electricity, the airplane, the computer's "productivity paradox"). By the time the macro data moves, the capability shift is long past. The alternative they propose is to measure the capability directly: how good is a model, today, at the actual work people are paid to do?

The trouble is that almost no benchmark looks like real work. The flagship evals — HLE, MMLU, GPQA — are academic tests engineered for reasoning difficulty, with a single checkable answer. The realistic ones are narrow: SWE-Lancer and the SWE-bench line live entirely inside software engineering. Neither captures the texture of a financial analyst building a model in Excel, a film producer cutting a trailer, a nurse drafting a care plan, or a mechanical engineer marking up a CAD file — multi-file, multi-modal deliverables where structure, format, aesthetics, and relevance matter as much as correctness, and where there is no answer key at all.

GDPval ("GDP" + "eval") is built to fill exactly that gap. It is top-down representative rather than convenient: start from the sectors that contribute most to U.S. GDP, descend to the highest-paid digital occupations within them, and source tasks that cover the majority of each occupation's Work Activities as tracked by the Department of Labor's O*NET database. The deliverables are real work product from professionals with, on average, 14 years of experience, and the headline metric — win rate against the human's own output — deliberately has no upper ceiling to saturate.

// how it works

The method

Picking the occupations. The authors selected the nine sectors contributing over 5% of U.S. GDP, then within each took the five occupations earning the most in total wages that are predominantly digital. "Digital" was operationalized the way Eloundou et al. (GPTs-are-GPTs) did: GPT-4o classified every O*NET task for an occupation as digital or not, weighted by O*NET's relevance/importance/frequency scores, and an occupation qualified if at least 60% of its task content was digital. The resulting selection was cross-checked against the Acemoglu–Autor task-content framework, where digital intensity correlated as expected — up with non-routine cognitive work, down with routine and manual work.

Building the tasks. Each GDPval task is a pair: a request (usually with reference files) and a deliverable — the work product. They are authored by recruited industry experts (minimum 4 years' experience, demonstrated promotion/management history, plus a video interview, background check, training, and quiz), drawn from prior employers including Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan, Boeing, Meta, Lockheed Martin, and Massachusetts General Hospital. Tasks are mapped onto O*NET occupational tasks for coverage, and each task's dollar value is estimated as average completion time × the occupation's median hourly wage. They are genuinely large pieces of work: an average of 7 hours of expert effort, up to multiple weeks on the high end, with up to 17 reference files per task in the gold subset (38 in the full set).

Quality control. All 1,320 tasks in the full set passed through an iterative pipeline of automated model screening and human expert review — an average of five reviews per task (minimum three), with reviewers leaving detailed comments and tasks revised between rounds. GDPval ships in two cuts: a held-out full set of 1,320 tasks (≥30 per occupation) and an open-sourced gold subset of 220 tasks (5 per occupation), the latter scrubbed of expert-identifying detail and released with a public automated grader.

Grading: pairwise comparison, not an answer key

Because there is no single correct deliverable, the primary metric is a blinded human pairwise comparison. An occupational expert sees the request and reference files, then ranks two or more unlabeled deliverables — one from the model, one the human gold standard — judging both objective correctness and subjective quality (structure, formatting, aesthetics). For the headline plots each model was sampled three times per prompt and each sample graded by three different experts — nine comparisons per prompt per model, across all 220 gold tasks. Grading a single comparison took, on average, over an hour.

The reported metric is the win rate w — how often the model deliverable is judged better than the human's. Because it is measured against a human baseline rather than a fixed key, it can't saturate at 100%: as models improve, the baseline can be swapped for a stronger one and evaluation continues.

w = # comparisons where model deliverable preferredtotal blinded pairwise comparisons // win rate vs. the human expert's own deliverable — "wins"; "wins + ties" also counts deliverables judged as-good-as the human

Expert grading is slow and expensive, so the authors also trained an experimental automated grader to mimic the expert pairwise judgment. It reaches 65.7% agreement with human expert graders — only about five points below the 70.8% inter-human agreement — and is offered publicly at evals.openai.com, though the paper is careful to call it a proxy, not a substitute.

// what they found

The results

Frontier models are approaching, but have not reached, parity with industry experts. On the gold subset, Claude Opus 4.1 was the best model: 47.6% of its deliverables were graded as better than (a win) or as good as (a tie) the human's — just short of the 50% parity line. GPT-5 high followed at 38.8% wins+ties, then o3 high at 34.1%, o4-mini high at 27.9%, Gemini 2.5 Pro at 25.5%, Grok 4 at 24.3%, and GPT-4o at 12.4%. The two leaders split along a clean axis: Claude excelled at aesthetics (document formatting, slide layout, .pdf/.xlsx/.ppt outputs) while GPT-5 excelled at accuracy (following instructions, correct calculations, pure-text tasks).

The trend line is roughly linear. Plotting OpenAI's own models by release date — GPT-4o (mid-2024) → o3 → GPT-5 (late-2025) — performance climbs steadily and nearly straight toward the parity line. The authors decline to over-extrapolate, but the slope is the paper's quietly load-bearing claim: capability on real economic work is improving at a predictable clip.

Why models lose

A clustering pipeline over the experts' written justifications shows the losses are mostly mundane, not deep. Grok 4 (35%) and Gemini 2.5 Pro (40%) lost overwhelmingly to instruction-following failures — promising but not delivering files, ignoring reference data, using the wrong format. GPT-5's profile is different: its dominant failure is formatting (10%), with the fewest instruction-following issues (9%). Accuracy errors (hallucinated data, miscalculations) were the smallest bucket for everyone (5–7%). The implication the authors lean on: many failures are cheap to fix.

"Just fix it" levers work. Increasing reasoning effort moved GPT-5 from 32.7% → 35.6% → 38.8% (wins+ties) across low/medium/high, with o3 tracking the same shape. A general formatting-and-thoroughness prompt — instructing the agent to render files as images and inspect them, avoid nonstandard Unicode, use LibreOffice for PDFs — plus best-of-4 sampling with a GPT-5 judge lifted GPT-5 another ~5 points to 43.1%. It eliminated black-square artifacts that had affected over half of generated PDFs, cut egregious PowerPoint formatting errors from 86% to 64%, and drove agents using their multimodal self-inspection from 15% to 97%.

The economics: real but modest

The flashy number is the "naive" ratio — a model produces a deliverable 90×–5,000× faster than the ~7-hour human. But that ignores quality. Under a realistic "try the model n times, review, and fix it yourself if it's not good enough" workflow, the payoff collapses to something sober. On the gold subset a human task averages 404 minutes / $361; reviewing a model deliverable adds 109 minutes / $86. Folding that in, GPT-5 yields about 1.39× speed and 1.63× cost savings, o3 about 1.28×/1.47×, and GPT-4o is actually worse than unassisted (0.46× speed) — its low quality means you mostly redo the work.

ModelWin rateSpeed (try-n)Cost (try-n)
GPT-4o12.5%0.46×0.53×
o4-mini29.1%1.06×1.22×
o335.2%1.28×1.47×
GPT-539.0%1.39×1.63×
// does it hold up?

The eval read

GDPval is the most economically grounded model eval yet, and the pairwise-vs-human design is genuinely clever — it sidesteps answer-key saturation and rewards the subjective qualities (format, aesthetics, relevance) that academic benchmarks throw away. But the headline "approaching parity" deserves to be read with several fingers on the scale.

The ground truth is itself noisy. The baseline is a single expert's deliverable, and the experts grading it agree with each other only 70.8% of the time. That ~29% disagreement isn't background — it's the same order as the gap being measured. In the failure analysis, when a fresh expert re-graded GPT-5's losses, 22.9% were flipped to "model actually better," almost exactly the inter-rater noise floor. So a meaningful slice of every win rate is grader disagreement, not demonstrated superiority; the error bars in Figure 5 are wide for a reason.

An OpenAI eval, imperfectly blinded, with asymmetric scaffolds. The authors admit samples were often identifiable — OpenAI outputs favored em-dashes, Claude wrote in the first person, Grok occasionally named itself — so human graders could infer provenance on a benchmark authored by one of the vendors. The scaffolds also differed by vendor: Claude was sampled through its UI to enable file-creation features, OpenAI models got web search, a code interpreter, and pre-installed libraries, and the team "were not able to obtain cost estimates for Claude, Gemini, and Grok," so the entire speed/cost analysis is OpenAI-only. To the paper's credit, the model that actually won was Claude Opus 4.1, which cuts against a simple self-promotion story — but the conditions are uneven enough that cross-vendor ordering should be held loosely.

The tasks are the easy version of the job. Every task is fully specified and one-shot: all context is in the prompt, with no back-and-forth, no figuring out what to do, no chasing down inputs. The authors' own "under-contextualized" experiment — prompts cut to 42% of their length — makes GPT-5 visibly worse. Real knowledge work is the underspecified version, so the headline numbers are best read as an upper bound that assumes the hard part is already solved.

Win rate ≠ can do the job. A deliverable that matches a human's on a clean, self-contained task is not the same as holding the role — there's no accountability, no multi-step workflow, no stakes. The severity breakdown matters here: among GPT-5's losses, 2.7% were "catastrophic" (harmful or dangerously wrong — a bad diagnosis, recommending fraud) and another 26.7% "bad." A few-percent catastrophic rate is a deployment-blocking number that a single averaged win rate hides entirely. And the automated grader — a GPT model judging GPT outputs — carries an obvious self-enhancement risk and is only released for part of the gold set.

baselines

A single human deliverable per task; cross-vendor models run under uneven scaffolds, and cost/speed numbers exist only for OpenAI models. Reasonable as a first cut, not as a leaderboard.

contamination

The 220-task gold subset is open-sourced, so future leakage is a live risk; the 1,320-task full set is held out, and win-rate-vs-human resists answer-key memorization better than fixed benchmarks.

what's missed

Catastrophic-error cost, interactivity, ambiguity-resolution, accountability, and ~99% of occupations. 44 occupations × 30 tasks is a thin, digital-only slice of the economy.

would raise confidence

Multiple human gold deliverables per task; vendor-matched scaffolds; interactive / under-specified tasks as the default; reporting catastrophic-failure rate alongside win rate; an independently-built grader.

// bottom line

GDPval is a real advance in making model evaluation economically legible — and its honest reading is more sober than "AI is approaching expert parity." On the cleanest, most generous version of knowledge work, the best model ties or beats a human just under half the time, against a baseline so noisy that a fifth of its "wins" are within grader disagreement, and a realistic human-in-the-loop workflow buys only ~1.4× speed. The trend line is the story; the level is not parity yet.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Measure capability, not adoption. GDPval evaluates frontier models directly on 1,320 real, paid knowledge-work tasks across 44 occupations in the 9 biggest U.S. GDP sectors — a leading indicator of economic impact rather than a lagging one.
  2. Grade by comparison, not by key. The primary metric is a blinded expert pairwise win rate against the professional's own deliverable, which captures formatting/aesthetics/relevance and can't saturate — but inherits ~29% inter-rater noise.
  3. Close, on the easy version. Claude Opus 4.1 reaches 47.6% wins+ties and GPT-5 high 38.8%, on a roughly linear upward trend — yet tasks are fully-specified and one-shot, ~3% of GPT-5's losses are catastrophic, and realistic AI-assisted workflows save only ~1.4× time. Approaching parity, not at it.
